December 5, 1990--' ';i 'S0
scott Hallabr i n
Acting GeneraL Counsel
California Fair PoIitical
Practices Corunission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 
95804-0807
Re: Request for confirming Letter
Dear Mr. Hallabrin:
I am an elected member of the Board of Directors of the
San Francisco Bay Area Rapid Transit District 
), a rapid
transit district existing under Sections 28,500 et seq. of the
California Pub1ic Utilities Code. I have been a board member
since ]974 and represent BART District No. 6 in southern Al-ameda
County.
I am the owner of a claims adjusting firm, John Glenn
Adjusters ("Adjusters") vrhj.ch operates in the San Francisco Bay
Area. Adjusters provides services to several transit providers
in the San Francisco Bay Area, including San Mateo County
Transit, Central Contra Costa ?ransit Authority, the Golden Gate
Bridge District and the Santa Cl,ara County Transit District.
Each of these transit companies has paid more than 9250 in fees
for serviees to Adjusters in the past tweLve months. Thus, f
consider that each of the transit companies is a "source of
income" to me as defined in the Political Reform Act and its
regulations. Adjusters' services consist of evaluating and
negotiating settlement of personal injury and property damages
claims asserted by members of the public. In large part, these
claims are rel-ated to motor vehi.cle collisions invol,ving buses
operated by the various transit companies. Claimants are usually
third partj.es, although bus riders comprise a significant number
of claimants.
Frotn time to time matters come before the BART Board of
Directors which may indirectly touch upon, in some way, the
business activities of a particular transit company which is a
source of income to me. !4y primary question concerns application
of the tests of foreseeability with respect to determining
\rhether there is a material financiaL affect on a source of
income. I rdould appreciate it if your response is in enough
detail to enable me to deal with particular questions whici are
bound to arise in the future as a resul"t of Eent,s corunitted and
proposed extension programs.


California FPPC
Re: Confirming Letter
Decembe r
, 1990
Potentialfy, any new or extended BART rail Line could
have an impact on any bus company operating in the vicinity of
the rail Line. If the bus services were comPeting with the rail-
services, there might be a detrimental impact at least on the
particufar bus route or routes where it could not compete. If
the bus company decided to operate service which was compli-
mentary to the rail transit service, by providing feeder service
to the rail stations, for example, the bus company's business
might improve.
Tero recent issues which came before the BART Board of
Directors will- il-lustrate, I hope, the nature of the problem. on
November 1, 1990 one of my fe11ow directors proposed that BART
subsidize certain bus service in the communiEy of Orinda. The
existing bus service provider, the Central Contra costa Transit
Authority (CCCTA), would have been asked to permit BART riders to
ride free to and from the BART station. 
BART would have, in some
manner, paid the amount of the fare for those riders. 
The
subsidy proposal was intended to entice casual car pool riders
not to park cars at the Orinda BART station. 
The proposal
involved onLy two months of service, on tr^ro of the cCcTA's bus
lines, and would have cost BART SL2,000.
CCCTA is a non-profit, public entity which operates
buses on about 25 routes, primarily in Contra Costa County. As
noted above, it is a source of income to me. In its fiscal year
1989, the CCCTA appears to have had gross annual rqgeipts in
excess of S10,000,000, but fess Ehan S100'000,000.12 CCCTA did
not initiate or participate in thj-s proposition, and there was
not contract before the Board of Directors.
r consulted BART's General Counsel- on this matter and,
given the proposed 9L2,000 expenditure, to be made in the next
t\,ro months, and using the yardstick of Section 18702.5(c) of the
regulatj,ons it vras clear that this would not have a material
financial affect on CCCTA. Accordingly, I voted on the
question. On similar matters, when the proposed action would
have an affect within the reasonably foreseeable future, and the
materiality of the financial affect can be measured, I would
expect to folLow the same anaLytical process. Please confirm
that this is appropriate, particularly the application of S
18702.5(c).
On the other hand, in October 1990 the Board proposed
to consider noving an undefined project for transit in the San
Ramon corridor (a geographic area running approximately 20 miles

By vray of background, the BART extension program is
Civided into three stages (copy attached). Stage I extension
projects.are 1ike1y to be completeC, al-though ful] funding is not
available at this time, and the conpLetion dates for the various
l-ines are betvreen 1995 and 2000. The Stage II Drojects, on the
other hand, are less defined, unscheduled and unfunded. Fina]Iy,
Stage III projects are largely undefined. In this regard, basj.c
decisions regarding the possibfe San Ranon corridor l-ine have not
even been made. Major delerminations, such as whether a heavy
rail (such as BART), or a light railltro11ey Lj-ne shouLd be used,
the Location of the route, and the number of stations are whoIly
up in the air. 
In my opinion, the only effect of the change in
staging for the San Ramon corridor project would be to notify the
public that BART was indeed interested in providing rail transit
in the corridor. 
That deciqjron, however, would not bring the
project closer to E;ffE ion. iZ
Horrever, because of the source of income relationship
with CCCTA, which operates one of its 25 bus routes in the San
Ramon corridor, I reguested clearance from BART's General
Counsel, as has been my pracLice.
The General Counsef suggested that I seek cLarification
from the Conmission on $Thether or not the possible financial
affect on CCC?A is foreseeable given the prel-imj,nary status of
this proposed project. 
Pending your response, I an not partici-
pating in discussions involving this matter.
In an attempt to analyze the materi.al,j.ty of any affect
on CCCTA, the General Counsel requested that the District's
planning stafE research the estimated financial impact on
CCCTA. Ho$rever, in order to consider this request the planning
staff found it necessary to make the following assumptions which
I believe are far too speculative to be foreseeable:
1. 
A San Ramon Valley l-iqht rail, Iine (a non-BART type
system to be operated by BART ) would operate from WaLnut Creek
BART to Dublin with its southern terminal near the proposed BIIRT

measure material.ity in Ehe distant future are appropriate at this

Therefore, with regard to the San Ramon corridor
staging decision, I request ansr,rers to the following:
1. 
Is a financial effect on CCCTA reasonably fore-
seeable from a decision to elevate the San Ramon corridor project
from Stage III to Stage II of BART's staging policy, notwith-
standing the fact that the earliest start of any construction is
probably 10 to 20 years in the future and subject to nunerous
contingencies in the planning and approval process, includj-ng
obtaining financing and the EIR process?
2. 
Is the materiality of any financial effect fore-
seeabLe from a decision to elevate the San Ramon corridor project
from Stage III to SEage II of BART's policy, notr^ri thstanding the
fact that any estimate of future effect will be projecting
conmencement of operations 15 to 25 years in the future and be
based on assumptions and predictions which are conjecturaL?
3. 
If the ansr.rers to I and 2 are affirmative, how can
you reconcile those anslrers vrith your advice to me in August 1988
(No. A-88-240) and your advice to former BART Director Robert
A1len in uarch 1982 (No. 82-1-004)?
I will be pleased to provide any further information
you require. Feel free to call or, if you prefer, I am willing
to meet with your staff and provide documentation and
information.
ART Director,

embedding.first_500_words: :-D:p -0ec R :,t December 5, 1990--' ';i 'S0 scott Hallabr i n Acting GeneraL Counsel California Fair PoIitical Practices Corunission 428 J Street, Suite 800 P. O. Box 807 Sacramento, California 95804-0807 Re: Request for confirming Letter Dear Mr. Hallabrin: I am an elected member of the Board of Directors of the San Francisco Bay Area Rapid Transit District ), a rapid transit district existing under Sections 28,500 et seq. of the California Pub1ic Utilities Code. I have been a board member since ]974 and represent BART District No. 6 in southern Al-ameda County. I am the owner of a claims adjusting firm, John Glenn Adjusters ("Adjusters") vrhj.ch operates in the San Francisco Bay Area. Adjusters provides services to several transit providers in the San Francisco Bay Area, including San Mateo County Transit, Central Contra Costa ?ransit Authority, the Golden Gate Bridge District and the Santa Cl,ara County Transit District. Each of these transit companies has paid more than 9250 in fees for serviees to Adjusters in the past tweLve months. Thus, f consider that each of the transit companies is a "source of income" to me as defined in the Political Reform Act and its regulations. Adjusters' services consist of evaluating and negotiating settlement of personal injury and property damages claims asserted by members of the public. In large part, these claims are rel-ated to motor vehi.cle collisions invol,ving buses operated by the various transit companies. Claimants are usually third partj.es, although bus riders comprise a significant number of claimants. Frotn time to time matters come before the BART Board of Directors which may indirectly touch upon, in some way, the business activities of a particular transit company which is a source of income to me. !4y primary question concerns application of the tests of foreseeability with respect to determining \rhether there is a material financiaL affect on a source of income. I rdould appreciate it if your response is in enough detail to enable me to deal with particular questions whici are bound to arise in the future as a resul"t of Eent,s corunitted and proposed extension programs. California FPPC Re: Confirming Letter Decembe r , 1990 Potentialfy, any new or extended BART rail Line could have an impact on any bus company operating in the vicinity of the rail Line. If the bus services were comPeting with the rail- services, there might be a detrimental impact at least on the particufar bus route or routes where it could not compete. If the bus company decided to operate service which was compli- mentary to the rail transit service, by providing feeder service to the rail stations, for example, the bus company's business might improve. Tero recent issues which came before the BART Board of Directors will- il-lustrate, I hope, the nature of the problem. on November 1, 1990 one of my fe11ow directors proposed that BART subsidize certain bus service in the communiEy of Orinda. The existing bus service provider, the Central Contra costa Transit Authority (CCCTA),
